<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577284 space=SYSML2P version=9 -->
## PAGE 00510: Constraints

- page_id: `249577284`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577284/Constraints
- version_number: 9
- version_date: `2025-10-13T15:40:45.405+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Common elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A constraint definition defines a logical condition, and a constraint usage applies this condition in different contexts, enabling the reuse of the constraint definition throughout the system. The constraint usage is satisfied when its expression evaluates to true, and violated when it does not.

##### Creating constraint usage and definition elements

###### Creating constraint usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

To create constraint usage and definition elements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. constraint def for a constraint definition.
  2. *constraint* for a constraint usage.
2. Specify the element name .
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.) in the element's body.
4. Click the Synchronize button.

constraintdefMassLimit{ // constraint definition 
attributemass:MassValue; 
attributemassLimit:MassValue; 
mass<=massLimit 
}

partvehicle{ 
attribute massActual : MassValue; 
attributemassRequired:MassValue;

constraintmassConstraint { massActual<=massRequired }// named constraint usage 
constraint{ massActual<=massRequired }// unnamed constraint usage 
}

###### Creating constraint usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

To create constraint usage and definition elements via the Create Element command

1. In the Containment tree, right-click an element, and in the shortcut menu, click **Create Element**.
2. In the Create Element dialog, select one of the following:
  1. constraint def to create a constraint definition element.
  2. constraint to create a constraint usage element.
3. Name the element.
4. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.).

###### Creating constraint usage and definition elements via the view palette

To create constraint usage and definition elements via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Requirements/Constraints group, click one of the following:
  1. The **constraint** def button to create a constraint definition element.
  2. The **constraint** button to create a constraint usage element.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the constraint.
3. Specify the constraint (expression, attributes, etc.).

###### Auto-creating constraint definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

To auto-create constraint definition elements via the Extract Definition commands

- See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extract Definition and Usage' space='']page.

## PAGE 00511: Control Functions

- page_id: `263094956`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/263094956/Control+Functions
- version_number: 10
- version_date: `2026-04-22T15:06:52.055+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model analysis > Functions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The table below lists the functions available in the*ControlFunctions*namespace of the Standard Libraries.

- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 

| Function | Example | Example (Osvaldo) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| dotabstract function . { in ref feature source[0..*] : Anything {chain}} | part point { attribute x = 3; attribute y = 4;}attribute result = point.x;Output>> result = point.x 3 | package ChainTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; part person { attribute age = 20; } attribute personAge = person.age;}Output>> personAge20 | Accesses a property or feature of a model element or structure. |
| ifabstract function if { in ref feature test[1] : Boolean in thenValue[0..1] in elseValue[0..1]} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; in thenValue = "Approved"; in elseValue = "Rejected"; if (x > 15) ? thenValue else elseValue}attribute a = 100;attribute result = Example(a);Output >> result = Example(a) Approved | package IfTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; attribute userAge = 17; attribute ifTest = if userAge > 18 ? "adult" else "child";}Output>> ifTestchild | Evaluates a Boolean condition and returns thenValue if condition is true; otherwise, returns elseValue. |
| ??abstract function ?? { in ref feature firstValue[0..*] : Anything in secondValue[0..1]} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; in y = "Default"; x ?? y}attribute a = null;attribute result = Example(a);Output >> result = Example(a) Default | TBDOutput | Returns x if it is not null; otherwise, returns y. Often used for defaulting values. |
| andfunction and { in ref feature firstValue[1] : Boolean in secondValue[0..1]} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; in y; x and y}attribute a = true;attribute b = false;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output >> result = Example(a, b) false | package AndTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; attribute travelerAge = 20; attribute hasValidPassport = true; attribute flightEligibility = if travelerAge >= 18 & hasValidPassport ? "clearedToFlyAbroad" else "notClearedToFlyAbroad"; }Output>> flightEligibilityclearedToFlyAbroad | Returns true if both Boolean expressions are true. |
| orfunction or { in ref feature firstValue[1] : Boolean in secondValue[0..1]} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; in y; x or y}attribute a = true;attribute b = false;attribute result = Example(a, b);Output >> result = Example(a, b) true | package OrTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; attribute travelerAge = 16; attribute hasParentalPermission = true; attribute flightEligibility = if (travelerAge > 17 \| hasParentalPermission) ? "clearedToFlyAbroad" else "notClearedToFlyAbroad"; }Output>> flightEligibilityclearedToFlyAbroad | Returns true if either x or y, or both, evaluate to true. |
| impliesfunction implies { in ref feature firstValue[1] : Boolean in secondValue[0..1]} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; in y; x implies y}attribute a = true;attribute b = false;attribute result = Example(a, b); Output>> result = Example(a, b) false | TBDOutput | Returns false only if the first Boolean expression is true and the second is false; otherwise returns true. Represents logical implication. |
| collectabstract function collect { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in mapper[0..*] { in ref feature argument[1] : Anything }} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c;}package Functions { private import ControlFunctions::*; calc def Example { in element : SysML::Package; element->collect { in x; (x as SysML::Package).ownedMember as SysML::PartUsage } } calc def ExampleAlternative { in element : SysML::Package; element. { in x; (x as SysML::Package).ownedMember as SysML::PartUsage } }}package Metrics { attribute result1 = Functions::Example(UserModel.metadata); attribute result2 = Functions::ExampleAlternative(UserModel.metadata);}Output>> result1 = Example(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a (46993027-ab5d-4fcd-bc08-d619efb9d45c) MetadataFeature PartUsage b (ac7a1d2e-ab80-415b-be93-294b787d97f1) >> result2 = ExampleAlternative(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a (46993027-ab5d-4fcd-bc08-d619efb9d45c) MetadataFeature PartUsage b (ac7a1d2e-ab80-415b-be93-294b787d97f1) | package CollectTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; package modelFragment { part engine; part gearbox; action assemble; } attribute nestedPartsInFragment = modelFragment.metadata->collect { in pkg : Package; pkg.member as PartUsage }; }Output>> nestedPartsInFragmentMetadataFeature PartUsage engine MetadataFeature PartUsage gearbox | Applies an expression to each element in a collection and returns a new collection of results (map operation). |
| selectabstract function select { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in selector[0..*] { in ref feature argument[1] : Anything }} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c;}package Functions { private import ControlFunctions::*; calc def Example { in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember->select { in x; x istype SysML::PartUsage } } calc def ExampleAlternative { in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember.? { in x; x istype SysML::PartUsage } }}package Metrics { attribute result1 = Functions::Example(UserModel.metadata); attribute result2 = Functions::ExampleAlternative(UserModel.metadata);}Output>> result1 = Example(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a (46993027-ab5d-4fcd-bc08-d619efb9d45c) MetadataFeature PartUsage b (ac7a1d2e-ab80-415b-be93-294b787d97f1) >> result2 = ExampleAlternative(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a (46993027-ab5d-4fcd-bc08-d619efb9d45c) MetadataFeature PartUsage b (ac7a1d2e-ab80-415b-be93-294b787d97f1) | package SelectTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; package modelFragment1 { part engine; action assemble; } package modelFragment2 { part gearbox; } attribute onlyPartsInPackage = (modelFragment1.metadata, modelFragment2.metadata)->select { in pkg : Package; pkg.member istype PartUsage }; }Output>> onlyPartsInPackageMetadataFeature Package modelFragment2 | Returns a collection of all elements that satisfy a Boolean condition (filter). |
| selectOnefunction selectOne { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in selector1[0..*] { in ref feature argument[1] : Anything }} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c;}calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::*; in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember->selectOne { in x; x istype SysML::PartUsage }}attribute result = Example(UserModel.metadata);Output>> result = Example(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a (a8e2a9ee-67f5-4f5b-81e8-4e2f5cd97e73) | package SelectOneTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; package modelFragment { part engine; part gearbox; } attribute onlyPartsInPackage = (modelFragment meta Package).ownedMember->selectOne { in member; member istype PartUsage }; }Output>> onlyPartsInPackageMetadataFeature PartUsage engine | Returns the first element in the collection x for which the selector1 condition evaluates to true. |
| rejectabstract function reject { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in rejector[0..*] { in ref feature argument[1] : Anything }} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c;}calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::*; in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember->reject { in x; not (x istype SysML::PartUsage) // remove non-parts }}attribute result = Example(UserModel.metadata);Output>> result = Example(UserModel.metadata) MetadataFeature PartUsage a (a8e2a9ee-67f5-4f5b-81e8-4e2f5cd97e73) MetadataFeature PartUsage b (7295f569-5c67-4376-ab8e-414d737f8749) | package RejectTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; package modelFragment { part engine; action accelerate; } attribute removeParts = (modelFragment meta Package).ownedMember->reject { in member; member istype PartUsage }; }Output>> removePartsMetadataFeature ActionUsage accelerate | Returns a subcollection of collection x excluding elements for which the rejector condition evaluates to true. |
| reduceabstract function reduce { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in reducer[0..*] { in ref feature firstArg[1] : Anything in ref feature secondArg[1] : Anything }} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c;}calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::*; in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember.name->reduce { in x; in y; x + y }}attribute result = Example(UserModel.metadata);Output >> result = Example(UserModel.metadata) abc | TBDOutput | Aggregates the elements of collection x by cumulatively applying reducer and returns a single result. |
| forAllabstract function forAll { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in test[0..*]} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c; attribute d;}calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::*; in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember->forAll { in x; x istype SysML::AttributeUsage }}attribute result = Example(UserModel.metadata);Output>> result = Example(UserModel.metadata) false | package forAllTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; part car { attribute tirePressureFront = 5; attribute tirePressureRear = 5; } attribute allTirePressuresAreNominal = (car.tirePressureFront, car.tirePressureRear)->forAll { in pressure; pressure == 5 }; }Output>> allTirePressuresAreNominaltrue | Returns true if the test condition holds for all elements in collection x. |
| existsabstract function exists { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Anything in test[0..*]} | package UserModel { part a; part b; item c; attribute d;}calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::*; in element : SysML::Package; (element as SysML::Package).ownedMember->exists { in x; x istype SysML::AttributeUsage }}attribute result = Example(UserModel.metadata);Output >> result = Example(UserModel.metadata) true | TBDOutput | Returns true if the test condition holds for at least one element in collection x. |
| allTruefunction allTrue { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; allTrue(x)}attribute a = false;attribute b = true;attribute c = true;attribute result = Example((a, b, c)); // collections must pass values in parentheses; Otherwise, the values are recognized as separate argumentsOutput>> result = Example((a, b, c)) false | package AllTrueTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; part car { attribute hasSpareTire = true; attribute hasFirstAidKit = true; } attribute hasRequiredSafetyItems = allTrue(car.hasSpareTire, car.hasFirstAidKit); }Output>> hasRequiredSafetyItemstrue | Returns true if all Boolean elements in collection x evaluate to true. |
| anyTruefunction anyTrue { in ref feature collection[0..*] : Boolean} | calc def Example { private import ControlFunctions::**; in x; anyTrue(x)}attribute a = false;attribute b = true;attribute c = true;attribute result = Example((a, b, c)); // collections must pass values in parentheses; Otherwise, the values are recognized as separate argumentsOutput >> result = Example((a, b, c)) true | package AnyTrueTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; part car { attribute hasSpareTire = false; attribute hasFirstAidKit = false; } attribute hasAnySafetyItem = anyTrue(car.hasSpareTire, car.hasFirstAidKit);}Output>> hasAnySafetyItemfalse | Returns true if at least one Boolean element in collection x evaluates to true. |
| minimizefunction minimize { in ref feature collection[1..*] : ScalarValue in fn[0..*] { in ref feature argument[1] : ScalarValue }} | part def Vehicle { attribute speed default 100; }part vehicle1 : Vehicle;part vehicle2 : Vehicle { :>> speed = 200; }attribute result = (vehicle1, vehicle2) -> ControlFunctions::minimize{ in x : Vehicle; x.speed}; Output>> result = (vehicle1, vehicle2)->minimize { in x : Vehicle; x.speed} 100 | package MinimizeTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; part drone { attribute leg1LengthCm = 32; attribute leg2LengthCm = 28; attribute leg3LengthCm = 35; } attribute shortestLandingLegCm = (drone.leg1LengthCm, drone.leg2LengthCm, drone.leg3LengthCm)->minimize { in x; x }; }Output>> shortestLandingLegCm28 | Returns the element of collection x for which the condition fn has the smallest value. |
| maximizefunction maximize { in ref feature collection[1..*] : ScalarValue in fn[0..*] { in ref feature argument[1] : ScalarValue }} | part def Vehicle { attribute speed default 100; }part vehicle1 : Vehicle;part vehicle2 : Vehicle { :>> speed = 200; }attribute result = (vehicle1, vehicle2) -> ControlFunctions::maximize{ in x : Vehicle; x.speed}; Output>> result = (vehicle1, vehicle2)->maximize { in x : Vehicle; x.speed} 200 | package MaximizeTest { private import ControlFunctions::*; private import SysML::*; part drone { attribute leg1LengthCm = 32; attribute leg2LengthCm = 28; attribute leg3LengthCm = 35; } attribute longestLandingLegCm = (drone.leg1LengthCm, drone.leg2LengthCm, drone.leg3LengthCm)->maximize { in x; x };}Output>> longestLandingLegCm35 | Returns the element of collection x for which the condition fn has the largest value. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577547 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00512: Control nodes

- page_id: `249577547`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577547/Control+nodes
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-22T13:44:08.236+02:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions > Controlling actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The sequencing of action usages is controlled using control nodes. Control nodes are always connected to other action usages by incoming and outgoing successions (with or without guards). Kinds of control nodes include the following:

- Fork node
  - Has one incoming succession and one or more outgoing successions.
  - The actions connected to the outgoing successions cannot start until the action connected to the incoming succession has completed.
- Join node
  - Has one or more incoming successions and one outgoing succession.
  - The action connected to the outgoing succession cannot start until all the actions connected to the incoming successions have completed.
- Decision node
  - Has one incoming succession and one or more outgoing successions.
  - Once the action connected to the incoming succession has completed, exactly one of the actions connected to an outgoing succession can start based on the guard condition of the outgoing succession that is satisfied (the else condition is the complement of all other guard conditions). See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Guard conditions' space=''] page.
- Merge node
  - Has one or more incoming successions and one outgoing succession.
  - The action connected to the outgoing succession cannot start until any one of the actions connected to an incoming succession has completed.

##### Creating control nodes

###### Creating control nodes via the Textual Editor

To create control nodes via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *fork*for a fork node.
  2. join for a join node.
  3. decide for a decision node
  4. merge for a merge node.
2. (Optional) Specify the node name .
3. Click the Synchronize button. actiontransportPassenger{ 
firststart; 
thenactionenterVehicle; 
thenactionstartVehicle; 
thenmergeNode; 
mergemergeNode;// named merge node declaration; it waits for the 'startVehicle'action to happen before continuingthenforkNode; 
forkforkNode;// namedfork node declaration; it enables all its successors to happen after it 
thenactiondriveVehicle; // the source of this succession is the fork node 
thenactionprovidePower;// the source of this succession is the fork node 
thenjoinNode; 
thenjoinNode; 
joinjoinNode;// namedjoin node declaration; it waits for all its predecessors to happen before continuing 
thenactionexitVehicle; 
thendecideNode; 
decidedecideNode; // nameddecide node declaration; typically followed by one or more conditional successions from which it chooses exactly one to happen after it 
ifanotherDestinationthenmergeNode; // references the merge node named 'mergeNode' as the target of the succession 
elsedone; 
}

###### Creating control nodes via the view palette

To create control nodes via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click one of the following:
  1. **fork**for a fork node.
  2. join for a join node.
  3. decide for a decision node
  4. merge for a merge node.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the node symbol.

style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">exitVehicle</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">       <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">decideNode</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">   <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">decide</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">decideNode</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">; <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">             </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">named </span>decide node declaration; typically followed by one or more conditional successions from which it chooses exactly one to happen after it </span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">       <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">if</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">anotherDestination</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">mergeNode</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;   <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// references the merge node named '<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">mergeNode</span>' as the target of the succession</span></span></span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">       <span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">else</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">done</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating control nodes via the view palette</h4><p>To create <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">control nodes via the view palette</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Actions group, click one of the following:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>fork </strong>for a fork node.</p></li><li><strong>join </strong>for a join node.</li><li><strong>decide </strong>for a decision node</li><li><strong>merge </strong>for a merge node.</li></ol></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the node symbol.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249577545 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00513: Controlling actions

- page_id: `249577545`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/249577545/Controlling+actions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-01-28T09:03:04.232+01:00`
- ancestors: SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > Model composition > Behavior > Actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can control actions using the following elements:

- **Start**and **done**actions that allow you to specify the start and end of an action sequence.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Start and done actions' space=''] page.
- Control nodes , such as decision, merge, fork, and join nodes that allow you to control the sequencing of action usages.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Control nodes' space=''] page.
- **Guard conditions**that allow you to specify conditional succession.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Guard conditions' space=''] page.
- Structured control actions , such as if action usage, while action usage, for action usage ****that allow you to control the performance of nested action usages in a structured way.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Structured control actions' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span>You can control actions using the following elements:</span></p><ul><li><span><strong>Start </strong>and <strong>done </strong>actions that <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">allow you to specify the start and end of an action sequence.</span></span><ul><li><span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Start and done actions" /></ac:link> page.</span></span></li></ul></li><li><strong>Control nodes</strong>, such as decision, merge, fork, and join nodes that allow you to control the sequencing of action usages.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Control nodes" /></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li><span><strong>Guard conditions </strong>that allow you to specify conditional succession.</span><ul><li><span>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Guard conditions" /></ac:link> page.</span></li></ul></li><li><strong>Structured control actions</strong>, such as if action usage, while action usage, for action usage<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span><strong> </strong>that allow you to <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">control the performance of nested action usages in a structured way.</span></span></span><ul><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Structured control actions" /></ac:link> page.</span></span></span></li></ul></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=296125574 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00514: COPY Activating style sheets

- page_id: `296125574`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/296125574/COPY+Activating+style+sheets
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-03-26T12:07:27.303+01:00`
- ancestors: COPY Symbol styles
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Whether you're using predefined style sheets provided by the modeling tool or custom ones you've created, you can manage which style sheets are activated for the entire project and which for specific views. This flexibility allows you to combine multiple style sheets based on your modeling needs, for example, activating a global style sheet for the entire project while activating distinct style sheets for individual views. Additionally, style sheets activated at both the project and view levels can be prioritized by setting their order of application. You can also disable project-level style sheets for a specific view.

Style sheets can be activated for:

- 
- 

##### Style sheets activation priority order

The order of priority in which the style sheets are activated for views:

1. The configurations for activating style sheets for individual views are of the highest priority. They are always of higher priority than the configuration for activating style sheets for the entire project.
  1. Take note of the type of style sheets you are using, i.e., whether [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Creating style sheets' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Creating style sheets' space='']. Those that reuse another style sheet inherit the latter's rules. This can result in unexpected usage of styles even if their style sheets are not activated directly. ExampleYou have a custom style sheet, *NewStyleSheet**,* which is created by subclassifying the *DefaultStyleSheet*. The *NewStyleSheet*contains several rules slightly modifying the *DefaultStyleSheet.*You activate *NewStyleSheet*for a specific view and *BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet* for the entire project.The specific view with the activated *NewStyleSheet*displays some symbols with the styles inherited from the *DefaultStyleSheet*, even though the latter is not activated for the project.
2. The ordering of style sheets for both individual views and the entire project is taken into account. If you specify multiple style sheets, order them based on your preferred priority, as the style sheets higher on the list take precedence over those lower on the list. Activating multiple style sheets is useful if the prioritized style sheet does not cover all available symbols. In that case, the secondary style sheet will cover those not covered by the first (if it contains the necessary rules).
3. The configuration for activating style sheets for the entire project is considered last.

##### Activating style sheets for the project

There must always be at least one style sheet activated for the project. If no style sheet is activated by the user, the default active project style sheet is *DefaultStyleSheet*.

###### Activating style sheets for the project via the Textual Editor

To activate style sheets for the project via the Textual Editor

1. Do one of the following:
  1. If the model already contains the root namespace dedicated to configurations that activate style sheets and creation dialogs, open it in the Textual Editor.
  2. If the model does not contain this namespace, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] and open it in the Textual Editor.
2. In the root namespace, declare a package named ProjectStyleConfig .
3. In the package's body, declare a part definition named myProjectStyleConfig and subset DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::StyleConfiguration .
4. In the part definition's body, declare a part and define it by the part definition of the style sheet you want to activate for the project, then subset activeStyleSheets .
5. Click the**Synchronize**button.

packageProjectStyleConfig{ 
partdefmyProjectStyleConfig:>DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::StyleConfiguration{ 
part:DS_Styles::StyleSheets::BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet:>activeStyleSheets; //*BlackAndWhiteStyleSheet* is activated for the project 
} 
}

###### Activating style sheets for the project via the Style Sheets button

To activate style sheets for the project via the Style Sheets button

1. Click the Style Sheets button in the view toolbar. In the dropdown menu, click Project Style Sheets command. The Project Style Sheets dialog opens.
2. On the left side panel of the **Project Style Sheets**dialog, select the style sheet(s) you want to use and click the **Add [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button.
3. Select a style sheet on the right side of the panel and click the **Up**or **Down**buttons to change the selected style sheet's priority.
4. Click the OK button. The selected style sheets are activated and element symbols are updated on project view s. Activating style sheets for the project via the **Style Sheets** button****automatically creates a new package *ProjectStyleConfig* with the active project style sheets configuration. The package is created in a root namespace dedicated to configurations that activate , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom creation dialogs' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enabling validation suites' space='']. If no such namespace exists in the model, it is automatically created with the *ProjectStyleConfig*package. The root namespace's name depends on the name of the package first created in the namespace.The procedure details the package's textual notation if you opt to create it manually.[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Activating style sheets for a specific view

To activate style sheets for a specific view, the view must be defined by a symbolic view definition.

- Symbolic views containing *Views::asInterconnectionDiagram* or *Views::asTreeDiagram* renderers, but [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating symbolic views' space=''], are automatically defined by *DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv* once you .
- If you are , you must first [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating symbolic views' space=''], e.g., *DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv.*

###### Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor

To activate style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, p lace the cursor within the body of the specific view whose style sheets you want to modify.
2. Declare a part and define it by the needed style sheet, then subset *explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets*.
3. (Optional) To i gnore the specification of active project style sheets for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view:
  1. Declare a part, redefine *effectiveStyleSheets*and set it to *explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets*.
4. Click the**Synchronize**button.

view'General View':DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv{

// ignores active project style sheets for the view, activating only those specified to be active for this view:part :>> effectiveStyleSheets = explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets;

// activating *NewStyle1*and *NewStyle2* style sheets for this specific view:

part:NewStylesPackage::NewStyle1:>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets; 
part:NewStylesPackage::NewStyle2:>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets;

// alternative notation for active style sheet specification for a specific view (equivalent to the above):

part:>>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets= (NewStylesPackage::NewStyle1(),NewStylesPackage::NewStyle2()); 
}

###### Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button

To activate style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button

1. Open the specific view whose style sheets you want to modify.
2. Click the Style Sheets button in the view toolbar . In the dropdown menu, click Apply Style Sheets command. The Apply Style Sheets dialog opens.
3. On the left side panel of the **Apply****Style Sheets**dialog, select the style sheet(s) you want to use and click the **Add [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button.
4. Select a style sheet on the right side of the panel and click the **Up**or **Down**buttons to change the selected style sheet's priority.
5. (Optional) Select the check box for the Ignore Active Project Style Sheets option. Selecting the option ignores the specification of active project style sheets specified in the **Project Style Sheets** dialog for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view in the **Apply** Style Sheets dialog .
6. Click the OK button. The selected style sheets are activated, and element symbols are updated in the view .

###### Displaying style sheets activated for a specific view

To display style sheets activated for a specific view

1. Open the specific view.
2. Click the Style Sheets button in the view toolbar . In the dropdown menu, click Display Applied Style Sheets command. Part definition element symbols of the style sheets activated for the view are displayed in the view. If style sheets are activated for the entire project, but none for the specific view, the command will not display the style sheet symbols.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Whether you're using predefined style sheets provided by the modeling tool or custom ones you've created, you can manage which style sheets are activated for the entire project and which for specific views. This flexibility allows you to combine multiple style sheets based on your modeling needs, for example, activating a global style sheet for the entire project while activating distinct style sheets for individual views. Additionally, style sheets activated at both the project and view levels can be prioritized by setting their order of application. containing <em>Views::asInterconnectionDiagram</em> or <em>Views::asTreeDiagram</em> renderers, but <ac:link ac:anchor="Creating undefined symbolic views using renderers via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[not defined by symbolic view definitions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, are automatically defined by <em>DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv</em> once you <ac:link ac:anchor="Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[activate a style sheet for the view via the Apply Style Sheets command]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </span></li><li data-uuid="5a15885c-fca8-423d-a253-314265872980"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">If you are <ac:link ac:anchor="Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[activating style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, you must first <ac:link ac:anchor="Creating symbolic views via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating symbolic views" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[define the view by a symbolic view definition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, e.g., <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><em>DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv. </em></span></span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To activate style sheets for a specific view via the Textual Editor</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="899d778a-80f9-4fce-8866-1d6fa1907550">In the Textual Editor, p<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">lace the cursor within the body of the specific view whose style sheets you want to modify.</span></li><li data-uuid="5721298b-65ea-43dc-a98f-e460141c5306"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Declare a part and define it by the needed style sheet, then subset <em>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets</em>.</span></li><li data-uuid="54ca3424-cd07-4396-b5bb-8a4dbeea95ee"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">(Optional) To i</span>gnore the specification of active project style sheets <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view:</span><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Declare a part, redefine <em>effectiveStyleSheets </em>and set it to <em>explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets</em>.</span></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="4b7ab0fd-af38-405d-9ada-4d30d62f6708"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Click the </span><strong style="text-align: left;">Synchronize<span> </span></strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">button.</span><br style="text-align: left;" /></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfd0c0d2-a5e0-47dd-b4a7-053712f2bc35"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">'General View'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> : </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">   // ignores active project style sheets for the view, activating only those specified to be active for this view:</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br />   <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span> :&gt;&gt; <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">effectiveStyleSheets</span> = <span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets</span>; </span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">   // activating <em>NewStyle1 </em>and <em>NewStyle2</em> style sheets for this specific view:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">: </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStylesPackage::NewStyle1</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">: </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStylesPackage::NewStyle2</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">;</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">   // alternative notation for active style sheet specification for a specific view (equivalent to the above):<br /></span></p><p><span>   </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">part</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">:&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">explicitlyAppliedStyleSheets</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> = (</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStylesPackage::NewStyle1</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">(), </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">NewStylesPackage::NewStyle2</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">());</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Activating style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To activate style sheets for a specific view via the Style Sheets button</span></p><hr /><ol style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="5f3368d5-98b9-487e-a6a1-099fb8cf0d6f">Open the specific view whose style sheets you want to modify. </li><li data-uuid="7ad8b0ed-a2f4-4284-a9db-ffa3650d842a">Click the<span> </span><strong>Style Sheets</strong><span> </span>button<span> in the view toolbar</span>. In the dropdown menu, click <strong>Apply Style Sheets </strong>command. The <strong>Apply </strong><strong>Style Sheets </strong>dialog opens.</li><li data-uuid="b44ee65b-1f0e-47f5-a783-ba0144024ff2"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">On the left side panel of the <strong>Apply</strong><strong> Style Sheets</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> dialog, select the style sheet(s) you want to use and click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. </span> </span></li><li data-uuid="b3a8e05d-0233-4b36-9291-f0a18b73f4db"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Select a style sheet on the right side of the panel and click the <strong>Up </strong>or <strong>Down </strong>buttons to </span>change the selected style sheet's priority. </li><li data-uuid="2b0faf88-b938-4413-b289-4f6507afcf9d">(Optional) Select the check box for the <strong>Ignore Active Project Style Sheets </strong>option. Selecting the option ignores the specification of active project style sheets specified in the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Project Style Sheets</strong> dialog for the selected view, only taking into account the style sheets specified for this exact view in the <strong>Apply </strong></span><strong>Style Sheets </strong>dialog<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span></li><li data-uuid="5c6eb371-5855-4c45-9aff-1c1b717d3d86">Click the <strong>OK</strong> button. The selected style sheets are activated, and element symbols are updated in the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">view</span>.</li></ol><h4>Displaying style sheets activated for a specific view </h4><p>To display style sheets activated for a specific view </p><hr /><ol style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="c17f76ac-863f-4acc-b8ac-b3e6b179213a">Open the specific view. </li><li data-uuid="21e469ca-c40e-4943-bb5d-8aebd2f15eef">Click the<span> </span><strong>Style Sheets</strong><span> </span>button<span> in the view toolbar</span>. In the dropdown menu, click <strong>Display Applied Style Sheets </strong>command.<br />Part definition element symbols of the style sheets activated for the view are displayed in the view.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a7c868e7-a07c-4944-b846-32c3c12da91b"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If style sheets are activated for the entire project, but none for the specific view, the command will not display the style sheet symbols.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304015599 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00515: COPY Creating custom tabular view definitions

- page_id: `304015599`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/304015599/COPY+Creating+custom+tabular+view+definitions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-05-06T16:58:44.798+02:00`
- ancestors: COPY Creating tabular views > COPY Custom tabular view definitions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Instead of, or in addition to, using the predefined tabular view definitions, you can create model-based custom tabular view definitions as part of your projects.There are two ways you can do that:

- Reuse predefined or custom tabular view definitions
- Create new customized tabular view definitions from scratch

You can customize the new view definitions to have specific tabular columns. Afterward, you can create individual view usages defined by the newly created tabular view definitions.

#### Creating custom tabular view definitions

##### Creating a custom tabular view definition

To create a custom tabular view definition

1. In the Containment tree, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing elements' space=''] with a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space=''] .
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing Textual Editor' space=''] for the new package and do one of the following:
  1. To create a new custom tabular view definition from scratch:
    1. Declare a view definition, specify its name, and subclassify the Base Table view definition from the DS_Views::CoreViews package.
  2. To create a new custom tabular view definition by reusing an existing tabular view definition (predefined or custom):
    1. Declare a view definition, specify its name, and subclassify the needed tabular view definition.
3. Within the body of the view definition, declare the keywords*render rendering*, (optionally) specify the name for the element,**and r edefine the inherited renderer. Since all standard predefined tabular view definitions use the *DS_Views::CoreViews::asTable***renderer, redefine the inherited *asTable* renderer for any new custom tabular view definitions created from scratch or those reusing predefined tabular view definitions.For a new custom tabular view definition that reuses a custom-created tabular view definition, check what the renderer of the latter is.
4. Click the Synchronize button. Proceed to .

The new view definition becomes available in theonce you:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating View Creation dialog' space=''].2. Add the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating View Creation dialog' space='']. This requires you to .

// ---------- view definitions

// a new view definition created from scratch:

viewdef'System Behavior Table':>DS_Views::CoreViews::bt{renderrenderingasNewTable1:>>asTable;}

// a new view definition created by reusing a predefined view definition *Generic Table*:

viewdef'System Components Taxonomy Table':> DS_Views::TabularViews::gt{ 
renderrenderingasNewTable2:>>asTable;

}

// a new view definition created by reusing a custom-created view definition 'System Behavior Table':

viewdef'Variant Table':> 'System Behavior Table'{ 
renderrenderingasNewTable3:>> asNewTable1;// *asNewTable3*redefines the *'System Behavior Table'* view definition's *asNewTable1* rendering, which itself redefines the *Base Table*view definition's *asTable* rendering

}

##### Modeling table columns

Once you have created the new tabular view definitions, you can model their columns. The procedures for tabular columns modeling are the same for both view definitions and view usages. Thus, see the procedures on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Modeling tabular view columns' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">DS_Views::TabularViews</span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">::gt</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">   </span><u><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">render</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">rendering</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> asN</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">ewTable2</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt;&gt; </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">asTable;</span></u></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">}</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// a new view definition created by reusing a custom-created view definition 'System Behavior Table':</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">view</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">def</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-teal,#206a83);">'Variant Table'</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> :&gt; <u>asN<span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">ewTable1</span></u></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">;</span></u><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">  </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">// <em>asNewTable3 </em>redefines the <em>'System Behavior Table'</em> view definition's <em>asNewTable1</em> rendering, which itself redefines the <em>Base Table </em>view definition's <em>asTable</em> rendering  </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Modeling table columns</h3><p>Once you have created the new tabular view definitions, you can model their columns. The procedures for tabular columns modeling are the same for both view definitions and view usages. Thus, see the procedures on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="COPY Modeling tabular view columns" /></ac:link> page.</p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=296125537 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00516: COPY Creating style sheet components

- page_id: `296125537`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/296125537/COPY+Creating+style+sheet+components
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-03-26T12:07:25.107+01:00`
- ancestors: COPY Symbol styles > COPY Custom style sheets
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

See the procedures on this page to create style sheet components.

#### Creating custom style sheet components

##### Creating global conditions for a style sheet

In addition to specifying conditions for individual rules in the style sheet (see procedure ), you can specify a global condition that applies to the entire style sheet. All individual rules declared in the style sheet apply only to the symbols that comply with the global rule.

###### Creating global conditions for a style sheet

To create global conditions for a style sheet

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Creating style sheets' space=''] .
2. Within the body of the part definition of the new style sheet, redefine the *styleSheetCondition*element inherited from the StyleSheet element and define it by the needed condition.
3. Within the body of the condition, declare a ref item , then r edefine the needed item and specify its values. The available ref items and their value types depend on the condition definition you are using. For a list of the condition definitions and their available ref items, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space='']. // the following style sheet contains a global condition 'General View Condition' followed by an individual rule for a specific element metaclass and applicable stylepackageNewStylesPackage{privateimportDS_Styles::**;privateimportCoreStylesComponents::*; 
 privateimportPredicates::*; 
 privateimportColorDefinitions::*; 
 privateimportCompartmentVisibilityDefinitions::*; 
 privateimportKerMLStyles::*; 
 privateimportSysMLStyles::*; 
partdefNewStyleSheet:>StyleSheet{ 
/* -------global condition -------*/ 
part'General View Condition':>>styleSheetCondition:ByDiagramTypeCondition{ // global style sheet condition targeting symbols by view definition 
refitem:>>diagramType=DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv.metadata; // the condition targets views defined by the *General View*view definition 
} 
/* -----------------------------------*/ 
partpartRule:>rule{ // an individual rule targets *PartUsage* metaclasses and applies the specified style to them. Because of the global rule, only part usages displayed on a General View will be affected by the rule 
part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view:ShapeStyle:>>style{ 
attribute:>>textColordefault"#2A16FF"; 
} 
} 
} 
}

##### Creating custom rules

###### Creating custom rules via the Textual Editor

To create a custom rule via the Textual Editor

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Creating style sheets' space=''] .
2. Within the body of the part definition of the new style sheet, s pecify the rule, d eclare a part usage, specify a name for the new rule, subset the rule element inherited from the StyleSheet element, and in its body, do any of the following as needed:
  1. .
  2. .
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space='']. packageNewStylesPackage{privateimportDS_Styles::**;privateimportCoreStylesComponents::*; 
 privateimportPredicates::*; 
 privateimportColorDefinitions::*; 
 privateimportCompartmentVisibilityDefinitions::*; 
 privateimportKerMLStyles::*; 
 privateimportSysMLStyles::*; 
partdefNewStyleSheet:> StyleSheet{/* -------new rule -------*/ 
partpartRule:>rule{// Specify the rule's condition 
 // Specify the rule's style 
}/* -----------------------------------*/ 
} 
}

###### Creating custom rules via the style sheet symbol compartment in a view

To create a custom rule via the style sheet symbol compartment in a view

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Creating style sheets' space=''] .
2. Create the rule part usage element for the style sheet's part definition element via the Create Element button by following the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compartments' space=''] procedure. A rule is created with the ByElementCondition condition and ShapeStyle style, which includes redefinitions for fillColor (with a randomly assigned color value) and penColor (with a comparatively darker color value) attributes. You can modify them as needed, see and . 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

partpart1:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::Predicates::ByElementCondition; 
part:>>style:DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::KerMLStyles::ShapeStyle{ 
attribute:>>fillColor="#81c784"; 
attribute:>>penColor="#3e6140"; 
} 
}

###### Creating custom rules via the Specification panel

To create a custom rule via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Creating style sheets' space=''] .
2. Create the rule part usage element for the style sheet's part definition element via the Specification panel by following the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] procedure. A rule is created with the ByElementCondition (Specific Elements) condition and ShapeStyle style, which includes redefinitions for fillColor (with a randomly assigned color value) and penColor (with a comparatively darker color value) attributes. You can modify them as needed, see and . 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

partpart1:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::Predicates::ByElementCondition; 
part:>>style:DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::KerMLStyles::ShapeStyle{ 
attribute:>>fillColor="#81c784"; 
attribute:>>penColor="#3e6140"; 
} 
}

##### Specifying conditions for a rule

You can specify the condition for a rule via the Textual Editor or the Specification panel. The names of the conditions differ based on the chosen specification method. See the comparison table below.

| Condition Element Name in Textual Editor | Condition Name in Specification Panel |
| --- | --- |
| ByElementCondition | Specific Elements |
| ByElementMetaclassCondition | Element Kind |
| ByMetadataAnnotationCondition | Metadata Annotation |
| ByDiagramTypeCondition | View Type |
| FreeFormCondition | Custom Expression |

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.

###### Specifying conditions for a rule via the Textual Editor

To specify a condition for a rule via the Textual Editor

1. Within the body of the part element of the rule, declare a part usage and r edefine the condition element inherited from the StyleSheet element, then define it by the needed condition.
2. Within the body of the condition, declare a ref item , then r edefine the needed item and specify its values.
  1. For the ByElementCondition , in its body, declare a ref item and redefine dependencyOwner . In its body, declare a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space=''] from the rule's condition to the target element.
  2. For the FreeFormCondition , declare a calc and redefine test , then specify the needed expression. The available ref items and their value types depend on the condition definition you are using. For a list of the condition definitions and their available ref items, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space='']. // *ByElementCondition* examplepartcustomRule:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::Predicates::ByElementCondition{ // the rule's condition is specified as *ByElementCondition*, targeting a specific element symbol 
refitem:>>dependencyOwner{ 
dependencyfromModel::CustomStyleSheet::customRule::conditiontovehicle; // the condition targets the *vehicle* element 
} 
} 
}// *ByElementMetaclassCondition* examplepartpartRule:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{// the rule's condition is specified as *ByElementMetaclassCondition*, targeting symbols by element metaclass 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; // the condition targets elements with the *PartUsage* metaclasses 
} 
}// *ByMetadataAnnotationCondition*examplepart metadataApprovalRule :> rule {part :>> condition : ByMetadataAnnotationCondition {// the rule's condition is specified as *ByMetadataAnnotationCondition*, targeting symbols by element metadata 
 ref item :>> annotatingMetadataType = 'Vehicle Metadata Definitions'::Approval.metadata;// the condition targets elements with the applied *Approval* metadata 
 }}// *ByDiagramTypeCondition*examplepart generalViewRule :> rule {part :>> condition : ByDiagramTypeCondition {// the rule's condition is specified as *ByDiagramTypeCondition*, targeting symbols by view definition 
ref item :>> diagramType = DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv.metadata; // the condition targets views defined by the *General View*view definition 
 }}// *FreeFormCondition*examplepartMyFreeformRule:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:FreeFormCondition{ // the rule's condition is specified as *FreeFormCondition*, targeting symbols by the specified expression 
calc:>>test{ 
'VCCU View'.metadata==diagram// targets element symbols on the specific view '*VCCU View*' 
 // ORelement== VCCU.metadata// targets the *VCCU* element symbol on all views (to which the style sheet is applied)} 
} 
}

###### Specifying conditions for a rule via the Specification panel

To specify a condition for a rule via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] for the style sheet's rule part usage element.
2. Specify the condition in the Specification panel's condition compartment:
  1. Specify the needed condition in the Match by field. Click the field to view the dropdown menu with available conditions, then click the needed condition to specify it for the rule.
  2. Specify the condition values in the value field. The value field name depends on the specified condition: ConditionValue Field NameDescriptionSpecific ElementsElementDrag and drop an element from the Containment tree or a view to specify the value.What happens in the Textual Editor: The feature value of the condition's item redefines *dependencyOwner*.Element KindMetaclassDrag and drop a metaclass (e.g. metaclass from KerML library or metadata definition from SysML library) from the Containment tree or a view to specify the value.What happens in the Textual Editor: The feature value of the condition's item redefines *elementMetaclass.*Metadata AnnotationMetadata DefinitionDrag and drop a metadata definition from the Containment tree or a view to specify the value.What happens in the Textual Editor: The feature value of the condition's item redefines *annotatingMetadataType.*View TypeView DefinitionDrag and drop a view definition from the Containment tree or a view to specify the value.What happens in the Textual Editor: The feature value of the condition's item redefines *diagramType*.Custom ExpressionExpressionSpecify the expression in the Textual Editor. [ATTACHMENT filename='condition specification.png']

##### Specifying styles for a rule

There are two ways you can specify a style for a rule:

- Local specification allows you to specify the style of a specific rule only.
  - See the procedures:
    - .
    - 
- External specification allows you to create a custom style definition outside the rule itself, which can then be reused for style specification of different rules.
  - See the procedure .

###### Specifying style attributes locally for the style of a rule via the Textual Editor

To specify style attributes locally for the style of a rule via the Textual Editor

1. Within the body of the part element of the rule, after the condition specification, declare a view usage, define it by the needed style definition, and redefine the style element inherited from the StyleSheet element.
2. Within the body of the view element, d eclare an attribute usage, redefine the needed attribute and specify its value. The available attributes depend on the style definitions (primary or custom)used for the view's definition:For a list of primary style definitions, their attributes, and value types, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.To create custom style definitions, see the procedure.To define by multiple style definitions, list them in a comma-separated list.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space=''].

part partRule :> rule {part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view:ShapeStyle:>>style{ // the rule's style is defined by a primary style definition *StyleShape* 
attribute:>>textColordefault"#FF0F37";// local modification of an available attribute's value 
} 
}

###### Specifying style attributes locally for the style of a rule via the Specification panel

To specify style attributes locally for the style of a rule via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] for the style sheet's rule part usage element.
2. In the Specification panel's style attributes compartment:
  1. Specify the style definition in the Type field:
    1. Click the input field to view the dropdown menu with available style definitions. Click the needed one to specify it as the type. [ATTACHMENT filename='style definitions dropdown menu.png']
    2. To remove the specified style definition, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='delete specified style definition button.png'] button next to the style definition. [ATTACHMENT filename='delete specified style definition.png']
    3. To remove all specified style definitions, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='More button for style definition.png'] button in the Type input field, then click Clear All . [ATTACHMENT filename='clear all types.png']
  2. Specify the style attributes:
    1. Modify the owned attributes in the Owned section:
      1. See the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] page.
    2. Redefine inherited attributes in the Inherited section and then modify them as needed:
      1. See the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] page.
      2. Then see the procedure [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification panel' space=''] page.

###### Hiding style attributes from the Symbol Style dialog for specific element kinds

To hide style attributes from the Symbol Style dialog for specific element kinds

1. Within the body of the part element of the rule, after the condition specification, declare a view usage, define it by the needed style definition, and redefine the style element inherited from the StyleSheet element.
2. Within the body of the view element, d eclare an attribute usage and apply the InvisibleInDialog metadata to it, then redefine the style attribute you want to hide from the Symbol Style dialog for the element kind specified in the rule's condition . The available attributes depend on the style definitions (primary or custom)used for the view's definition.For a list of primary style definitions and their attributes, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.For more information on the Symbol Style dialog, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Modifying styles of individual symbols' space=''] page.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space=''].

part partRule :> rule {part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view:ShapeStyle:>>style{ 
#InvisibleInDialogattribute:>>contentFillColor; // the style attribute *Content Fill Color* is hidden from the Symbol Style dialog for part usages} 
}

###### Creating custom style definitions

Unlike local attribute specification, creating custom style definitions allows you to reuse them for style specification of multiple rules.

You can create custom style definitions specifying the needed attributes and their values. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Custom style sheets' space=''] must subclassify [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Custom style sheets' space=''] (such as *ShapeStyle, ShapeOnBorderStyle*, etc.), inheriting their attributes, which you can modify. However, you can only add attributes that are available to the primary style definition that the custom style definition subclassifies. 
For an extensive list of primary style definitions and their available attributes, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] page.

To create a custom style definition

1. Within the body of the part definition of the new style sheet, d eclare a view definition element, specify its name, and subclassify a primary style definition from the DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::KerMLStyles package. For a list of primary style definitions, their attributes, and value types, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.
2. Within the body of the view definition element, d eclare an attribute usage, r edefine the needed attribute and specify the attribute's value. The available attributes and their value types depend on the primary style definition you are using.For an extensive list of the primary style definitions and their available attributes, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space='']page.
3. Specify the custom style definition for the style of a rule.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space=''].

partdefNewStyleSheet:>StyleSheet{ 
partpartRule:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ // custom rule 
ref item :>> elementMetaclass = SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view : PartTextStyleRule :>> style; // the style of the rule is updated to be defined by the new custom style definition 
} 
viewdefPartTextStyleRule:>ShapeStyle{ // new custom style definition subclassifies the primary style definition *ShapeStyle* 
attribute :>> textColor default "#68FF70"; 
} 
}

###### Creating custom reusable style attribute values

You can create custom style attributes whose values you can reuse by referencing them where needed. Additionally, you can create custom style classes grouping such attributes.

To create custom reusable style attribute values

1. Declare an attribute, s pecify its name, then define it by the needed value type, and s pecify its value.
2. (Optional) To group attributes within a custom style class, declare a part usage, specify its name, then place the attributes within the part's body.
3. Reuse the newly created style attribute values for or . Use [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing textual notation' space=''] for attributes nested within .
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space=''].

partdefNewStyleSheet:>StyleSheet{ 
partpartRule:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view:PartTextStyleRule:>>style; // the custom style is passed to the rule's style 
} 
viewdefPartTextStyleRule:>ShapeStyle{ 
attribute:>>penColordefaultcustomColorPalette.customDarkGreen; // the custom color from a custom style class is reused in a custom style definition 
} 
partcustomColorPalette{// custom style class with reusable style attributes for custom color values are specified 
attributecustomDarkGreen:Colordefault"#357F35"; 
attributecustomLightGreen:Colordefault"#BFF7AA"; 
} 
}

##### Specifying the compartment style of a rule

To specify the compartment style of a rule

1. Within the body of the view element, declare a part usage, specify its name (optional), then d efine it by the appropriate compartment style definition. To determine which primary compartment style definitions are available for each element symbol, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] page.
2. Within the body, redefine the needed compartment style attribute followed by the appropriate attribute value. **** To determine which attributes and what value types are available to each primary compartment style definition, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Style sheet conditions and styles tables' space=''] page.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='COPY Synchronizing custom style sheet modifications' space='']. partpartRule:>rule{ 
part:>>condition:ByElementMetaclassCondition{ 
refitem:>>elementMetaclass= SysML::Systems::PartUsage.metadata; 
} 
view:ShapeStyle:>>style{ // defined bya primary style definition 
attributeCompartment :AttributesCompartmentStyle{ // attribute for the attributes compartment's 
:>>showCompartmentdefaulttrue; // modifying the *showCompartment* value 
} 
} 
}
